title: 2026/04/02 - JU [Part 4]
displayTitle: 2026-04-01 - Justice Committee - Department of Corrections, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2026 [Part 4]
topic: Department of Corrections, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2026
description: Thursday, 2 April 2026 | Justice Committee Department of Corrections, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2026 Hearing of Evidence Department of Corrections Jeremy
descriptionSummary: Department of Corrections, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2026

chunks.text: Yes. But anyway, congratulations for that. Now, we've got a bit of time with you this morning. Looking forward to hearing what you've got to say. As a committee, as you know, we love asking questions. I have become accustomed to that reality, but you come to me just to make some, for this occasion, very short remarks, just to try and frame the conversation and shape some of the questions. So thanks, firstly, for inviting us to talk to you about the long-term insight briefing. And I think you also wanted to maybe spend a short amount of time on the Mendeima's order. Yes. We've got one member particularly interested.

Everyone should be interested if you need to talk about the legal situation. So we asked you a bit. Awesome. So I'll cover that in my opening remarks just in terms of framing it, because I am able to talk about it to a level and then there are some limitations. But can I just make sure I've introduced my colleagues listening at the table? You are familiar, I think, with Dr Anita Ryan, Deputy Chief Executive of my community's partnerships and pathways, and Tom Baker, who's a Chief Digital Officer. So they're both here. I've seen the Chief Digital Officer's role here is not needing to be explained. And Anita obviously brings lots of insight around the regulatory and integrative services, where we think it's a good opportunity. And also at the moment, it's covering the community partnerships and pathways space, where also technology has a quite

significant role to play. Corrections, LTB on potential technology changes, looks over the next 25 years and focuses on areas where technology could potentially help to address issues that may arise in the future. So very importantly, framing this, this is future focused, long-term in outlook to help us plan. Technology is already an important part of our operations. I think we've spoken in the past about corrections officers already utilising body wall cameras for body scanners, having been introduced at some of our prison sites and what that's then enabled in terms of reducing strip searching, enabling same activity to be done in a much less intrusive way, both for those in our care, as well as the officers who work in that environment. Other technologies including virtual reality for training purposes

have been trial successfully in some sites. Prisons though, and I think this is the important point to set context, prisons specifically are by design and necessity environments, often built with steel reinforced concrete, particularly in the high security maximum security environment. And therefore introducing new technology, particularly on existing assets can be difficult and expensive, sometimes making hearts take full advantage of all available technology, both now and into the future. Despite that, we are focused on having a plan for how that can be introduced progressively, taking advantage of technology where it makes sense, but is also affordable. The LTIB looked at three key areas where technology could be used in the future to add value or improve efficiency or safety, those being in prisons, in the community,

and as I've said in the rehabilitation or integration space. I thought that would be a helpful focus into those three areas because that's kind of how we thought about it in the LTIB. Before I finish, just on the specifics of the mandamus order, just again to help frame what I will be able to talk about rather than what is still subject to proceedings, I will just briefly talk on mandamus order and the work that is underway as a result. It's important to note that the order arose during existing proceedings and while it's somewhat independent, I'm going to caveat some of my answers given the wider matter is still subject to say. So I'll just be clear about where those two things sit and where I want to maybe take a step back from the specifics and give you context rather than answering any specific

questions. The order requires that I as Chief Executive am required to comply with Section 70 of the Corrections Act in the Administration of Conditions, specifically of detention in units 12 and 13 at Auckland Prison. That's the maximum security environment and the order arose during proceedings focused on two units at Auckland Prison related to Section 70 of the Act which provides the detailed amendment type of exercise. In short, the judgment touched on several issues, including not consistently meeting open air requirement often due to infrastructure constraints, the conflation of exercise and time out of cell and that inadequate resourcing was not a lawful basis for denying minimum entitlements. We have responded to this so it was important that we heard, understood and then took action around the issuing of

that mandamus order responding by increasing resourcing, looking at new approaches to unlock regimes to meet the requirements of the order and I can confirm that the circumstance of the game rise to the order have not occurred since those changes have been made. So I'll give that as a frame to hopefully then enable as much of a constructive conversation as possible whilst not travelling into the lives of... This is nothing to appeal, is that what you're saying? That's what I was wondering. No, sorry it is and we are not appealing that at all. So are you finished now? Yes, I am. Right, I'm going to go to Timothy Paul, you first to ask the questions. Yeah, can we just clarify that because before we did a bit of research about whether this was still ongoing and our understanding was it was corrections, was it applied for an appeal and

that was denied by the judge and now that it just is settled? We are not, we're not appealing the mandamus order sands but it is still part of a wider, I think the term is interlocutory set of matters that is still a live case in the High Court. So that's the interrelationship between the two. There's an order, that is something that we had to apply and comply with from the end of last year and I can talk to that but I'm not going to talk about any of the wider implications of the case because that would be subject to you to say. Yeah, okay. So you go? Yeah, so over the last two years this committee and a few briefings and during scrutiny week have asked yourself and your leadership whether corrections is meeting minimum entitlements

and from my recollection it was always Lee Marsh who would answer a lot of the minimum entitlement questions. Every time corrections said to us that you guys were meeting the minimum entitlements so I was a bit of a shock to see the High Court order last year. So what genuine assurances can corrections give to our committee that this is not just going to be a slap on the wrist for Auckland Prison? What are you doing to oversee the fulfilment of those entitlements in every single prison and what would be the consequences for the operation of Auckland Prison not meeting statutory obligations? Correct. Okay, well I'll I think take a step back in

terms of the specifics of the mandamus order which was related to units 12 and 13 of Auckland Prison rather than any far any sort of wider findings. The context of those two units as you'll be aware I think you've visited that environment is that they are managing the most complex violent people in our system and within that context we have put in place regimes that try and prioritise what is in our guiding principles of the Corrections Act under Section 6 maintaining public safety as our primary and paramount consideration in decision making. What that meant for us on a day-to-day operational basis prior to the mandamus order was that we were very much wanting to and attempting to achieve minimum entitlements

particularly the one-hour access to fresh air every day and what we were discovering every day that was that the nature of the volatility of the individuals and the care required to ensure we were not inappropriately mixing prisoners in their open air space to avoid serious assaults and harm of prisoner on prisoner as much as the potential for serious assaults to our officers that we were seeing incidents that then enabled us to apply an exception to achieving minimum entitlements under 69 to B and C which are enabling us for reasons of security of prison the prison operations being threatened or health and safety of any person in the prison

being threatened lawful reasons for why we would in those situations not apply minimum entitlements. Our step back working through this case and responding to the order has been for us to confront what we I think had drifted into given the complexity of the people and our desire to achieve that paramount consideration of public safety we had probably normalized as poor regime routine in those two units exceptions almost every day as a result of concerns of threats to safety and health and safety what now having engaged with the order and having reflected on the judge's clear consideration and therefore issuing of something that is you know obviously a

very substantive position is that we needed to take a step back and say well what do we need to do to ensure that we can still account for the safety of our staff who do incredibly difficult work every day with those most violent people in our system but still accounts for that as the norm because that is that is what that unit is achieving and we need to address the resourcing of people to enable us to operate regime that normally can achieve those minimum entitlements so that was the big change for us in mindset that we weren't operating on a default of well this is just this is just the resourcing that we've applied historically to running a maximum security environment we now need to think well what additional resources do we need so what

I can confirm as a result of that ruling that order we've invested two million dollars per year in additional frontline staff capability specifically to those units that has increased the amount of officers who are available increased the number of movements that can occur has anticipated the complexity of the interrelationships and has made a safe working operation that now properly sees exception rather than default being threat of prisoner safety or health and safety concerns every day so I can confirm that we are now recording every day with absolute consistency the adherence to an achievement of minimum entitlements and where that is not achieved and I think it's important to note because some days it won't be we've got really

clear documentation that 69 to b or c one or other has applied that then justifies why and we're explicit that that is not as a result of us not adapting and adjusting our staffing and operating regime regime and I think to your broad question of how can we be assured that doesn't apply elsewhere this particular part of our network and our system is it you know if you can make a triangle of highest risk most complex right at the top this is right at the top of that organizational construct and therefore I am assured because we now have that same record of minimum entitlement achievement across our network that this was the exception and this was the area where we had prioritized as paramount requirement public safety and that had allowed

us to drift into not saying actually things have changed the complexity in nature of the prisoners has become more unpredictable more violent and we now need probably to consider different resourcing to one where the operations weren't weren't kind of putting their hand up we unfortunately needed this order to allow us to properly go in and say so it's got a change we need to adopt a roof regime allows us more normally to maintain those minimum entitlements you got another question well I mean I was keen to dunk on me oh just suck on that yeah it's just that last comment maybe really rose an eyebrow which was we needed this order you knew once it was drawn to your attention and it clearly was prior to proceedings being filed you knew you weren't complying with illegal

obligations why did it why did it require a judge a hearing and a judge to say do what the law says to for the corrections to respond in the way it has if I'm a our view of our position was that we were lawfully applying the intent of the act I referenced the section six principles the paramount consideration being public safety above all other things and therefore it does it did need to be tested and so from our perspective so your view if I've got the case right your view was you were having resourcing constraints which meant you couldn't safely deliver under section 70 and that was justified is that essentially in a nutshell your case

that is a that is a simplified version of how we were operating the site we had considered the risks and the considerations of how we achieved safe operations in that particular unit those two two units and our response was to ensure safety with the paramount consideration and how we then worked our way through that was ensuring that we could confirm that first before anything else and that was the way in which officers in the in the line of fire so to speak were considering their ability to weigh on a case by case day by day basis whether or not what they were experiencing with a particular prisoner being particularly aggressive or particularly violent on that day amounted to a justification for exception two and that just

gave us the opportunity to reflect having heard from a judge how they had interpreted the law right and because there are exceptions in cases of emergency and so on to not comply with and and threats to safety of prisoners or staff and threats to health and safety but what has been proven by your the case and your actions is that properly resourced this was a systemic problem of under resourcing rather than some ongoing emergency. That's a question. Well I would be happy to debate that in terms of until we got that crystallized for us our proposition was we were weighing two very complex and careful things about what is the bar what are the standards of 69 to BNC in every single circumstance for every single prisoner in that most complex space

and whether or not and I think this is what I've tried to convey whether or not in each circumstance we had drifted into a mode where we were almost assuming because of the people who were managing there that every day we were going to have a set of circumstances where there would be exceptions that would mean minimum type of that's what I meant. From what I heard you say before my read why not you said before it's different entries in the interpretation is that you hadn't the way you are operating you you had interpreted the law to be be such that you could operate the way you operate right correct and so up to the point of the time the judge came up with his opinion it was that that point is that what

because that's what I thought you was exactly it's exactly it's exactly what I was trying but now what you've said is now that you recognize you obviously recognize the decision you're spending too many in a year to find right okay yeah I'm making my question wrap into the long term because like I appreciate the answer but this is not the first time that corrections has been challenged over the provision of minimum entitlements because during COVID obviously there was a crisis that justified the reduction of meeting those targets but when we moved out of lockdown and when it was safe to go back to normal corrections were not I think it was at Arohata women's prison were not meeting minimum entitlements so this is not the first time that this has come up which is what makes

me wonder whether this is a system-wide issue and from my observations I wonder whether this is going to the long term but hang on just please let me finish it seems to me that a lot of the resourcing is going towards increasing capacity of our prisons and managing the increase in prison population which has increased by nearly a thousand people during your tenure as chief executive of corrections as opposed to the quality of providing the basic minimum entitles entitled to every prisoner regardless of the severeness or seriousness of their offender what you are describing is the period at the end of during and then at the end of COVID where I think I have come to this committee in its various forms and acknowledged and

talked about the weighing of public safety being paramount our needs to ensure that we had sufficient staff who were able to then undertake safe work as we were transitioning and experiencing high turnover the need to recruit as demand increased and how we managed those tensions across our network so what what I would acknowledge is as a result of context changing and our need to be able to account for ourselves absolutely explicitly that we were achieving minimum entitlements which historically were just that we are you know for for the previous period from 1995 until 2022 23 there was there was no explicit documented position that was assuring everyone that every

single person was achieving every single minimum settlement in every single day but for the exceptions we are now in a position where COVID and the period out required us to be able to evidence that we were achieving those things and what we are now this is where the technology kind of link comes in we are now in a mode where we have the opportunity starting in unit 12 and 13 at Auckland prison to start to remove the need for officers to be tied up using their own documented approach to recording all of these things and try and prioritise their time being connected to those in our care rather than documenting those things so we are not in a

place where it's a it's a live thing yet but what we have seen in this work is humans undertaking lots of documented record keeping is not the most effective and efficient beneficial use of their time where technology can play a part potentially is the use of CCTV camera footage the connection of that back into our systems and that's become more of an automated approach to that absolute credible auditable document documented record freeing up officer time so that's that's for us what I think extending our existing technology to something that will help us in the future reduce the the administrative burden on our officers free them up to spend more time

and create an even stronger evidence trail than the date that we have today because it's a human based system rather than a machine based system at the moment can I just I won't give back the members original question and I think she's the second time she's asked the question what what this committee wants to be assured is as of today can you give assurance to the committee that the minimum standards of whatever minimum size whatever way you do it where that's for you but the committee wants to be assured that you are meeting those and if you're not we would love to know where you have areas of concern and by when you expect to address it I

think that was what member was originally asking about that so I can keep it short but I can keep it short and say yes we are achieving those and our assurance process for that is we now have measures for every single member entitlement for every single prisoner at every single site and that is being reported on a daily basis so what all I'm reflecting on in terms of then moving into the technology is that is a resource administratively burdensome part of an officer's day which is important that we can do from accounting for ourselves and demonstrating adherence but technology in the future has probably got a really important part to play in being able to automate that part of what our responsibilities are to then free back up

officer time which I think is far more appropriately spent engaging with those in our care trying to get them on a more positive pathway so they leave us better with broader prospects so that's the that's the that's the link if you like between an important conversation and okay I'm going to go to a new line of questioning now to Mr Todd Simpson well thank you all kind of pick up because I think obviously you said technology focus in that insights briefing long term it's briefing community prisons rehabilitation I think we've covered prisons a bit but keen to understand a bit more yeah what you're thinking in the rehabilitation space because yeah kind of what did you uncover in putting together the briefing around how technology could help because obviously yeah we want to make sure that there is opportunities for people that here in

good care to get rid of yeah yeah so so I think acknowledging in my setup that there are really material barriers to just introducing what we might all view as normal technology into into prison context that the hard infrastructure part we have significant pilot examples where the use of things like virtual reality which has been used particularly for the education part of our services is a really valuable vehicle to drive engagement to drive connection to something beyond the institutional setting and can can really give a lift to the educational attainments

that would otherwise perhaps not be as beneficial so I think our reflection is virtual reality is a really valuable tool that's been particularly effective I say in education and training giving people access to some certification qualification that otherwise might not be as practical within a prison context and it's shown real gains in terms of those things I'll turn to my colleagues who are probably much more knowledgeable on this but I would say what we have seen in other jurisdictions is a move to then include more than education and training within that virtual reality space and that for me is quite an interesting area to watch because I you know at a human level I can perhaps appreciate with what is possible now with virtual reality

the opportunity to move people out of what they would have as their day-to-day world is something very different which might be super helpful in helping them shift their behaviors and mindset so that might be an interesting area to focus on that one might have a view and then Tom from a from a delivery perspective and then the second bit is secure online learning and the ability to ensure that we are keeping abreast with how training and education is being delivered with those normal politic type courses and it's been critical for us to be able to shift our mode to enable more and more access of an online nature rather than what has historically been necessary to be our model face-to-face delivery and that is becoming less and less

the mode for some of that delivery but I don't know whether you've got any reflections when either around virtual reality broadening those to other services what you might have as a perspective in terms of how that might work with kind of evidence and practice in some of those services because they'll be pros and cons right yeah there will be and in terms of virtual reality within a that the offense focus rehabilitative context I'm actually not aware of how that is being utilized overseas specifically but I do know virtual reality more widely for mental health services for some forms of specific illnesses that people might have has been trialled from probably the late 90s in New Zealand around how we support people to overcome

some of those within kind of real time or real looking contexts so some of that would be particularly useful for us to consider in a prison context where it's also very challenging for people to be in the real life context and the relevance of that to us I think is that that helps set people up for mental health barriers that are really challenging them to engage in offense focus rehab proper as Jeremy said in terms of virtual reality for education we've had some really good trials of this within the Department of Corrections and with some really good outcomes in terms of people being able to achieve some new CA level results not across the spectrum of or across the suite or across the country but certainly something that we are seeing some new green shoots from so I'm going to Dr Tracen McLenna thank you Mr Chair I made a couple of questions and Tom

you're probably best place to answer and because the long-term briefing is really no free lunch is here Tom but the briefing is really interesting so I'm pleased that you're turning your mind to all of the you know the potentials out there but plans it's really important that the plans remain adaptable as you'll know because rather than being sort of locking in systems that are quickly going to be outdated so I'm assuming that that's forming part of your thinking as well and also so that's one question and also noting that you know the report talks about how new tech can change how crime is detected and managed so have you also considered whether that sort of increased use of tech could itself drive changes in prison demand over time I

don't know if that was sufficiently sort of taken into account and okay that's through those two we're running out of time a bit yeah my interactions with my peers and colleagues not just in New Zealand but in Australia suggest that not really in reduction of crime however there is a view around how we could detect just certain interactions between individuals within the custodial environment and then what could come about as a result of the data that then falls out of that that we could then leverage you know for prisons operations perspective because you can imagine a situation where internal charges like changes the nature of of of teaching what you're monitoring isn't it yeah absolutely um do you just want to touch on I think the first question was sort of pushing into the

how do you maintain progress long-term view of where you're trying to get to without what you're doing becoming outdated before before you've even done it did I feel some of that in your question that sort of speaks to the three phases of your digital SLI yeah yeah yeah so our focus in our digital statement intent is about strengthening and transforming and so what what we're attempting to do with that is stay connected to again my peers both here and not just in the public sector the private sector but also across the ditch in Australia so on behalf of corrections I chair the emerging technologies our group of people that sit across the corrections environment and we meet quite regularly every quarter and the things that we talk about and discuss is the emergence of

technology and the longevity longevity of that in terms of its use within our environment so we focus on particular areas topics that we've touched on virtual reality we have talked about that in the education space that has limited time we've talked about AI seems the topic this year of the day and we know that that in and of itself will have limited time span as well so we're trying to think beyond that what what are the next things that come along that we might have to turn our minds to in terms of supporting our particular function in our environment and and I think you know think think about our integration for management system which is which is our core platform where data exists to give us information about every single person in their

care case-noting ability to search document that's a legacy legacy system an old system which has been maintained over time we're part of a wider justice system as you well know so the interrelationship between a police system a justice system and a correction system that need to seamlessly interact but are at different stages creates a sort of similar tension for us in terms of when do we when do we start thinking about what might be next as a different legacy system versus what do we need to do today to maintain stabilised but sort of improve and enhance the existing system so I think IOMS is a really good example of how we get confidence and assurance that we have a stable platform whilst Teldeca is being introduced as the case management system

within the courts part of justice so that we are ready with a stable platform to then start to consider how we might shape our future systems as that starts to come in to perhaps think about and I think this is this is a really important principle that we've used as a department we have to know that we have been really disciplined in a way in which we articulate the operational activities that we want to happen in a particular way the workflow before we start ambitiously describing what the technology needs to be so until we describe the workflow activities that will help that work achieve our outcomes we're not yet ready for the technology so we're putting the effort into that whilst Teldeca is coming on so that we're in a

position to we're running out of time I'm going to have two more questions one more from Tracey yeah so I and that largely answered it so so I assume making sure that you're able to measure and monitor correct so that it's in service of rehab and reduce reoffending not just throughput correct okay ten was it Paul that's all right so like long-term looking at the makeup of the prison population accepting you know that you don't make the law there has been accepting all of that the proportionate Māori in prison is still worryingly high it seems that attempts or efforts to refresh whakai rangi despite this committee recommending that

have not gone anywhere and I'd love to hear an update if that's if I'm wrong on that the there was a decision made to remove the Māori leadership advisory board to your leadership and and then to look at woman the woman's prison population that's shot up recently under your leadership there have been the Waikete riots the escape high-profile escape of Philip Smith and other really serious incidents and incidents of escapes resulting in innocent members of the public being long-term insights so long-term insights long-term insight how are you going to change all of that because it's not a great record okay I am that's not even touching on the internal allegations of bullying and internal cultures

that is in the media right now yeah I mean that's a that's a lots of questions I mean my my short answer to that is we work in a very complex part of the wider system of public services I am incredibly proud of the work that everyone undertakes every day within corrections context we are but one important part of a whole heap of different agencies and parties that together leads to the consequences that you have described we have been committed to ensuring that we are confronting some of the realities in our part of the system you mentioned the bullying matters and I reference our approach to speaking up standing up sexual harassment being an absolute focus for us and the over 2,300 people within

our organization have already been through really extensive training not because we've told them they must but because they have determined that it's the right thing to do as a result us pushing in and saying it's incredibly important for us more than anyone else to lead well and to be very conscious of decency and dignity in in all of our work so I just part that particular point in there and I think to your questions around Horkaidengi addressing the over the temptation of Mardi and particularly around women it is a long-term intractable challenge that is clearly not a corrections alone issue we though have heard and understand our role and responsibility in ensuring that we don't sit on our hands and do nothing and I think between yourself and other members historically of this

committee we have been challenging ourselves as to whether or not targets or areas of focus are important to shift us away from not being able to apply clear operationally relevant change to some of these matters and so to that end just last week we had a pretty engaging conversation by that I mean constructive tension in an executive context to ask ourselves were we super clear on where we thought we could have the biggest impact to ensure that we were confident that we were doing all we could both in our agency as well as across the sector to understand what is a small part but an incredibly important part of the growth in the women's prison population which is those women who are coming into our part of the system

on relatively relatively low-level offenses that may have been recidivist in nature and so that's an area that you have asked us about I think the chair had a particular interest in that when we were last talking about about this as a sector and we have definitely enrolled our colleagues in justice to understand the data to understand what's happening as this term what we can practically do that actually starts to demonstrate that we can make a difference at least in a small part rather than just keeping it in frameworks descriptions and intent so that that is my reflection it's incredibly rewarding work incredibly difficult work but I'm 100% proud of what I know to be the commitment that every single member of our organization has in trying to make a difference and enable people to leave us in a better state

with broader prospects but it's a tough job but I'm delighted that we've got incredible people doing that work every day hey well thank you for coming and I can assure you what we will end with in our report is that we look forward to hearing your recommendations your new approach blah blah blah what you can see thank you but hey look I want to thank you for your role I know you're moving on but also to your team thank you we all recognize exceptionally challenging environment you operate in but just thank you for coming in today appreciate it thank you thank you Mr. Chair Mr. Chair yeah sorry could we just what do you think about just staying in public session and getting on with the hearings and coming back to consideration on the slide I'm just going to look at the officials can you come back um do you mind waiting just fight for this yeah do you mind waiting and then we'll they don't they don't need to let afterwards say

yeah we don't we don't have officials on that okay all right thank you great so if we just right we're going straight everyone we're going straight into uh a hearing thank you my list is to Piri Piri um Raquel uh Private Authority that's you fantastic please um come to the table looking forward to hearing from you we're running just a little bit late um we've got five minutes with you we've read your submission um so if you can just leave us a little bit of time for questioning that would be fantastic um

thank you my name is Hita Gardner I am here on behalf of the Piri Raquel Tribal Authority as our kaiho too the Piri Raquel Tribal Authority strongly opposes this bill that is our view that this bill is a solution looking for a problem it offers no practical benefit but carries significant constitutional and cultural risk English is already the dominant language of our courts our schools commerce and in our streets around 95 percent of people here in

New Zealand speak English compared to 4.28 percent that speak Maori and 0.5 percent of the population reported to be using sign language according to our most recent census data 95 versus 4.28 versus 0.5 the English language is not at risk so what problem are we actually solving it was the Maori language that was removed from early childhood education material it is the use of Maori language that has been reduced within government departments and in some cases removed from correspondence with international partners the Maori Language Commission

has warned the Maori language is at risk and just this very week this week the Maori language voices on our airways have been diminished with substantial funding cuts there is a genuine concern for ewe radio that is the Maori language that is under attack and a bill to affirm the status of the already dominant language and now there was a waste of time and a waste of resource and a blatant example of political Austria unlike te reo Maori and New Zealand sign language English English is not vulnerable and does not require legislative protection elevating it to official

status doesn't improve public services it simply dilutes the specific protections meant for at-risk languages te reo Maori is a taonga under article 2 of the treaty or Waitangi the crown has a duty of active protection not just passive tolerance when one language is still recovering from state sponsored suppression legislating the dominance of the already dominant language is inconsistent with the principles of te te reo Waitangi this bill doesn't sit in a vacuum it's part of a broader shift towards linguistic monoculturalism it signals to Maori that our language and our place in public administration

is becoming at best optional or secondary and at worst now surplus to requirements the bill states that legislative recognition of the status of English as an official language will not affect the status or use of te reo Maori and New Zealand sign language as the two other official languages in New Zealand will not affect the status or use of te reo Maori that's just not true anti-te reo Maori sentiment is already been emboldened by government legislation we will have dialogue suggesting we don't need to consider te reo Maori because the we use English and that is already an official language of New Zealand that is the type of thinking that will infect

our society of this legislation is to pass and to think that might be the case is naive can bust the opinions of those individuals that support this bill and you'll find that very sentiment is prominent in the thinking so what problem are we actually solving? Pederaco tribal authority steadfastly opposes the English language bill thank you very much i've got Dan Rose one who's going to ask you a question yeah hey thanks for that contribution i was just wondering if there's been any international examples where you're seeing where similar legislation has improved public or administration outcomes and if you have kind

of what lessons could we potentially take from that sorry could you could you ask the question yes any examples internationally that have actually where a film like this has actually improved public outcomes i'll see you mentioned here about the the danger of following the US model has there been other other similar bits of legislation that have led to it being better for the lives of the of that nation or so having a bill like this improving things yeah have you seen so the like the opposite or no okay can i ask you a question that's very good submission in the historical perspective is fascinating but you mentioned the treaty settlement process and the obligations on the crown to up here to a whole up hold to rail you make the assertion that
